title: Communication Disorders Laboratory
description: Supervised therapy for students with speech, voice, language, or hearing problems. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for credit. Grade: Pass/Not Pass (P/NP).

title: Concepts in Communication Sciences
description: Concepts essential in understanding human communication; factors affecting life-long development and competency of speech, language and hearing.

title: Introduction to Phonetics
description: Recording of speech using the International Phonetic Alphabet in broad transcription. General American dialects and variations. Prerequisites: Majors only, or by permission of instructor. Corequisites: Must be taken concurrently with CSD 307 , CSD 308 , and CSD 309 . Notes: Not open to freshmen or sophomores.

title: Speech and Hearing Science
description: Acoustic principles of speech and hearing; analysis of the acoustic characteristics of speech and physiological correlates; speech perception. Prerequisites: Majors only, or by permission of instructor. Corequisites: Must be taken concurrently with CSD 306 , CSD 308 , and CSD 309 . Notes: Not open to freshmen or sophomores.

title: Language and Speech Development
description: Theory and evidence of the chronological development of phonology, syntax, semantics, and pragmatics in the child. Prerequisites: Majors only, or by permission of instructor. Corequisites: Must be taken concurrently with CSD 306 , CSD 307 , and CSD 309 . Notes: Not open to freshmen or sophomores.

title: Anatomy and Physiology of the Speech and Hearing Mechanism
description: Anatomical and physiological bases of human communication. Prerequisites: Majors only, or by permission of instructor. Corequisites: Must be taken concurrently with CSD 306 , CSD 307 , and CSD 308 . Notes: Not open to freshmen or sophomores.

title: Independent Study SLP  1-3
description: Directed study and/or research under faculty supervision. Prerequisites: Permission of the instructor. Notes: May be repeated once for credit for a total of 6 s.h. Grade: Pass/Not Pass (P/NP).

title: Introduction to Audiology
description: Basic anatomy and physiology of the human auditory system, fundamental hearing science, and methods and techniques of hearing measurement and interpretation for the assessment, diagnosis, evaluation, and rehabilitation of hearing disorders. Prerequisites: Speech Pathology/Audiology and Interpreting, Deaf Education, and Advocacy Services majors only.

title: Independent Study Audiology  1-3
description: Directed study and/or research under faculty supervision. Prerequisites: Permission of the instructor. Notes: May be repeated once for credit for a total of 6 s.h. Grade: Pass/Not Pass (P/NP).

title: Speech Sound Disorders
description: Assessment and treatment procedures of speech and sound disorders. Prerequisites: CSD 306 , CSD 307 , CSD 308 , and CSD 309 . or permission of instructor.

title: Language Disorders
description: Nature, theory, measurement, and management of language problems across the life span. Prerequisites: CSD 306 , CSD 307 , CSD 308 , CSD 309 .

title: Voice and Fluency Disorders
description: Basic theories and principles in the onset, development, and maintenance of stuttering and voice disorders in children and adults. Primary factors in prevention, measurement, assessment, and management. Prerequisites: CSD 306 , CSD 307 , CSD 308 , CSD 309 .

title: Diagnostic Procedures: Inquiry, Observation and Measurement
description: Processes and techniques of data acquisition and analysis for the diagnosis, assessment, and evaluation of communication disorders. Prerequisites: Completion of all CSD 300-level courses required for the major or permission of the instructor.

title: Communication and Aging
description: Development of communication in old age; factors affecting development and competency; communication evidence and theories of aging; facilitation of life-long functional communication. Prerequisites: CSD 308 or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Speech Science
description: Acoustic theory and methods of analysis; acoustic structure of speech and its physiological correlates; application of acoustic information to clinical management of disorders of communication. Prerequisites: CSD 306 , CSD 307 , CSD 308 , CSD 309 .

title: Aural Rehabilitation
description: Principles of aural rehabilitation with hearing impaired adults and their significant others. Prerequisites: CSD 334 .

title: Pediatric Aural Rehabilitation
description: Study of new technologies available for children with hearing impairment and the impact of these technologies on therapy and teaching. Prerequisites: CSD 334 for undergraduates. permission of instructor for graduate students.

title: Honors Clinical Practice in Speech-Language Pathology
description: CSD 471 offers supervised clinical experience in the treatment of communication disorders to select undergraduate students majoring in Speech Language Pathology and Audiology (SPAU).

title: Instrumentation for Communication Disorders
description: Instrumentation commonly used in communication disorders; operation and measurement techniques for clinical and research applications. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Structured Clinic Observations for Speech-Language Pathology Students
description: This course is designed to satisfy the American Speech-Language-Hearing Association requirement for 25 guided clinical observation hours in speech-language pathology. Prerequisites: CSD 334 , CSD 336 , and CSD 337 . or permission of instructor; Notes: Grade: Pass/Not Pass (P/NP).

title: Structured Clinic Observations for Audiology Students
description: Students interested in graduate studies in the field of Audiology will be a part of in person clinical observations of the prevention, identification, diagnosis, and evidence-based treatment of hearing and other auditory disorders. Prerequisites: CSD 334 , CSD 336 , and CSD 337 . or permission of instructor; Notes: Grade: Pass/Not Pass (P/NP).

title: Introduction to Adult Language Disorders
description: Assessment, treatment, and quality of life issues associated with acquired cognitive-linguistic disorders in adults will be addressed. The targeted disorders include aphasia, dementia, and traumatic brain injury.

title: Neurology of Speech, Language and Hearing
description: An overview of neuroanatomy and neurophysiology with a concentration on neurological mechanisms related to speech, language and hearing. Prerequisites: CSD 309 or permission of instructor.

title: Senior Honors Project
description: Independent original scholarship, completed under the supervision of a faculty member. Culminates in an original oral presentation, written document, or other creative work. Prerequisites: Senior status and admission to the Lloyd International Honors College and the CSD Honors Program, or permission of the instructor. Notes:May be repeated once for credit.

title: Honors Independent Study  3-6
description: Directed study and/or research under faculty supervision. Prerequisites: Senior status and admission to the Lloyd International Honors College and the CSD Honors Program, or permission of the instructor. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Special Topics Seminar
description: Critical analysis of contemporary topics in Communication Sciences and Disorders. Prerequisites: CSD 334 , CSD 336 , and CSD 337 , or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for credit twice during the senior year of UNCG undergraduates. Second degree students may enroll twice during the year they are taking undergraduate courses.

title: Diagnostic Procedures: Inquiry, Observation and Measurement
description: Processes and techniques of data acquisition and analysis for the diagnosis, assessment, and evaluation of communication disorders.

title: Aural Rehabilitation
description: Principles of aural rehabilitation with hearing impaired adults and their significant others.

title: Pediatric Aural Rehabilitation
description: Study of new technologies available for children with hearing impairment and the impact of these technologies on therapy and teaching. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Introduction to Adult Language Disorders
description: Assessment, treatment, and quality of life issues associated with acquired cognitive-linguistic disorders in adults will be addressed. The targeted disorders include aphasia, dementia, and traumatic brain injury.

title: Neurology of Speech, Language and Hearing
description: An overview of neuroanatomy and neurophysiology with a concentration on neurological mechanisms related to speech, language and hearing.

title: Professional Issues and Ethics in Speech-Language Pathology
description: Issues related to the profession, service delivery, ethics, legal considerations, funding issues, program administration and credentialing. Content is in accordance with the American Speech-Language-Hearing Association Scope of Practice. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Special Topics in Communication Sciences and Disorders   1-3
description: Topics in communication sciences and disorders. Prerequisites: Admission to M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for credit when topic varies.

title: Seminar in Voice Problems
description: Investigation of current literature and theories of vocal anomalies. Organic deviations. Prerequisites: CSD 551 or equivalent and admission to appropriate degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Fluency Disorders
description: Assessment, diagnosis, and treatment of fluency disorders in children, adolescents, and adults. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Seminar in Speech and Language Pathology
description: Studies dealing with specialized areas of communication disorders. Topic to be announced each semester course is offered. Prerequisites: Graduate-level courses in appropriate content area and students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for credit when topic varies.

title: Cognitive-Linguistic Disorders in Adults
description: Assessment, diagnosis, and treatment of communication disorders resulting from stroke, dementia, and traumatic brain injury. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Augmentative and Alternative Communication   1-2
description: Knowledge of augmentative and alternative communications and problem-solving in the design and application of appropriate AAC systems. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Language Learning Disabilities
description: Assessment and treatment of spoken and written language learning disabilities in school-age children and adolescents with special emphasis on the collaborative role of the speech-language pathologist in school-based settings. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Motor Speech Disorders
description: Neurologic basis, assessment and treatment of dysarthria and apraxia. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: The Supervisory Process in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Study and practice of clinical supervision in speech-language pathology. Theoretical models of supervision, pertinent research, and clinical practice. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Identification and treatment of speech and language differences in diverse populations. Prerequisites: Admission to the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology or permission of instructor.

title: Cognitive and Linguistic Disorders in Adults
description: The purpose of this course is to provide the requisite knowledge required to understand, evaluate, and treat acquired adult cognitive-linguistic disorders including traumatic brain injury, right hemisphere disorder, and dementias. Prerequisites: CSD 606 or permission of instructor.

title: Dysphagia
description: Anatomical bases of normal and disordered swallowing in children and adults; evaluation and treatment of swallowing disorders. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Introduction to Research in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Introduction to research procedures in the study of communication sciences and disorders. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Special Projects in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Individual scholarly project developed by the student with faculty guidance. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor. Notes: Grading method is Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory (S/U).

title: Prevention, Assessment, and Intervention of Communication Disorders: Birth-5
description: Communication development and disorders (speech, language, and hearing) in infants, toddlers, and preschoolers. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Prevention, Assessment, and Intervention of Communication Disorders: School Age
description: Communication development and disorders in school age children and adolescents. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor.

title: Minor Area Clinical Practicum for Speech-Language Pathology Majors
description: Instruction for speech-language pathology majors in the minor area of hearing assessment. Provides training and supervised clinical experiences in hearing screenings. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program.

title: Independent Study   1-3
description: Topic to be determined by faculty advisor and student. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor and major advisor.

title: Clinical Foundations in Speech-Language Pathology I
description: Instruction in the clinical process for providing evaluations and treatment for individuals with communication disorders or differences. Corequisites: CSD 671L or permission of instructor.

title: Clinical Practice I
description: Clinical practicum experience in providing screenings, evaluations and treatment for patients or clients with communication disorders or differences. Corequisites: CSD 671 or permission of instructor.

title: Internship in Communication Disorders   3-9
description: Clinical experience under supervision of a certified speech-language pathologist in an off-campus facility. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program.

title: Clinical Foundations in Speech-Language Pathology II
description: Instruction in specialized topics related to contemporary professional practice and use of instrumentation and technology for evaluating and treating individuals with communication disorders or differences. Prerequisites: CSD 671 , CSD 671L . Corequisites: CSD 678L or permission of instructor.

title: Clinical Practice II
description: Clinical practicum experience in providing screenings, evaluations and treatment for patients or clients with communication disorders or differences. Prerequisites: CSD 671 , CSD 671L . Corequisites: CSD 678 or permission of instructor.

title: Service Learning and Clinical Practicum in International Settings
description: Students will compare and contrast professional issues in global settings through guided study-abroad experiences, service learning experiences and interaction with students and teachers abroad while gaining clinical practicum hours. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program.

title: Thesis  1-6
description: Individual guidance in the development of a specific research problem. Prerequisites: Students enrolled in the M.A. in Speech-Language Pathology program or permission of instructor and major advisor.

title: Professional Seminars in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Weekly seminar on contemporary issues in communication sciences and disorders. Notes: May be repeated four times for credit. Grade: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory (S/U).

title: Advanced Seminar in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Critical analysis of special topics in communication sciences and disorders. Topics change each semester. Notes: May be repeated when topic varies for a maximum of 18 hours.

title: Best Practices in Teaching Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Introduction to best practices in teaching in communication sciences and disorders. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor for non-CSD majors.

title: Practicum in Teaching Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Practicum in teaching in communication sciences and disorders. Prerequisites: CSD 771 (or as corequisite). prior permission of instructor for non-CSD majors; Notes: Course may be repeated once for credit. Grade: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory (S/U).

title: Independent Study   1-3
description: Guided readings, research, and individual project work. Prerequisites: Permission of doctoral advisory committee and graduate faculty mentor. Notes: May be repeated for a maximum of 15 hours credit.

title: Directed Research  1-3
description: Individual work on directed research or topics of special interest, collection and analysis of data, critical review, integration, and interpretation of research literature on a topic pertinent to the student's research. Prerequisites: Permission of doctoral advisory committee and graduate faculty mentor. Notes: May be repeated for a maximum of 15 hours credit.

title: Dissertation   1-12
description: Prerequisite: Completion of doctoral examinations.

title: Dissertation Extension  1-3
description: Dissertation Extension.

title: Research Extension  1-3
description: Research Extension.

title: Introduction to Communication Studies
description: Students will learn to construct and deliver coherent oral arguments for various contexts. Principles of competent communication will be addressed. Recordings will be used to evaluate and enhance communication skills. MAC: MAC Oral Communication

title: Great Conversations about Democracy
description: Course content explores the theory and practice of collaborative dialogue, deliberation, and decision-making in the American Democratic tradition. This includes analysis and deliberation over the foundational documents of our democracy, and with contemporary issues. Students will be trained in conducting reasoned, reasonable, and persuasive oral and written communication. MAC: MAC Civics and Community

title: Introduction to Communication Inquiry
description: This course prepares students to ask and answer questions from a communication perspective. Students will engage critical thinking skills to evaluate evidence, interpret communication journal articles, and package information.

title: Communication and Community
description: Exploration of role and impact of communication in diverse communities. Ethical and social responsibilities of civic action are examined in the context of community problem solving. Includes service learning experience in a supervised setting.

title: Interpersonal Communication
description: Contemporary theory and practice of relational communication, with emphasis on increasing awareness of strategic and ethical uses of communication to build relationships. MAC: MAC CritThink Soc and BehavSci

title: Organizational Communication
description: Examines contemporary organizational communication theories and practices as they enable students to function, change, learn, and create/recreate within and across a variety of organizations.

title: Communication Inquiry
description: Communication Inquiry prepares students to ask and answer questions from a Communication perspective. The course focuses on information literacy, communication theory and practice, and synthesizing research to advance ideas. Lectures will emphasize how theory assists us in understanding, predicting, and transforming society. Prerequisites: CST 105 or permission of instructor. Notes: Students cannot receive credit for both CST 298 and CST 300 .

title: Communication Theory
description: Critical analysis and evaluation of scientific, rhetorical, and critical theories of communication. Emphasis on how theory assists us to understand, predict, and transform society. Prerequisites: Junior status and above and any two courses from among CST 200 , CST 207 , and CST 210, or permission of instructor.

title: Persuasion in Western Culture
description: The history of rhetoric (persuasion) and its evolution in Western culture, from ancient Greece to our current age. Application of rhetorical theory/criticism to various historical and contemporary communicative events. Prerequisites: CST 105 and junior standing.

title: Communicating Ethically
description: Provides students with an opportunity to think critically about ethical and moral dimensions of current practices in interpersonal, institutional, and public communication.

title: Dialogue, Deliberation, and Decision Making
description: Course content explores the theory and practice of collaborative dialogue, deliberation, and decision-making. This includes analysis and deliberation over contemporary issues and training in reasoned, persuasive oral and written communications.

title: Persuasion and Social Influence
description: Theories and practices of persuasion in critically evaluating and creating/composing persuasive messages. Role of ethics in relational, group, and mass mediated persuasive communication. CIC: CIC College Writing

title: Digital Content Creation
description: This course teaches students to create engaging and ethical digital content across multiple genres, preparing them for professional communication in a rapidly evolving digital landscape.

title: Special Problems  1-3
description: Guided individual study in an area of special interest to the student. Prerequisites: Permission of faculty supervisor. Notes: May be repeated for credit.

title: Intercultural Communication
description: Drawing from multiple theoretical perspectives, this course explores theories, research, and issues important to the understanding of communication between people from different racial, ethnic, national, and other cultural backgrounds.

title: Communication and Workplace Relationships
description: Advanced communication skills applied to the workplace. Emphasis on both oral and written communication in interviewing, making presentations, and creating ethically grounded professional communication.

title: Communication and Public Relations: Strategies and Innovations
description: Prepares students for effective and ethical public communication and relations on behalf of profit and not-for-profit organizations. Students conduct research, apply strategies, and utilize innovative methods to meet organizational communication objectives. CIC: CIC College Writing

title: Crisis Communication
description: Investigates the range of crises facing us in every segment of society. From readings, case studies, videos, and presentations an understanding of current communication functions relating to these issues are studied. CIC: CIC College Writing

title: Conflict Communication
description: Course explores how communication is central to expressing opposing voices and managing conflicted relationships, change, diversity/difference, and identities. CIC: CIC College Writing

title: Small Group and Team Communication
description: Theory and practice of small group/team communication, emphasizing student participation. Develops skills for leadership in small group/teams. Develops framework for analysis of effective small groups/teams.

title: Speaking Center Theory and Practice
description: Explores principles of Communication Across the Curriculum, applying them to interpersonal communication, listening, group communication, public speaking, and pedagogy to prepare Communication Consultants in UNCG's Speaking Intensive program. Prerequisites: CST 105 or CST 341 (may be taken as a corequisite). 3.0 GPA in the student's major; written permission from the Speaking Across the Curriculum Center Director.

title: Communication Research Methods
description: Theoretical examination and practical application of the philosophical, ideological, and processual bases for selecting, using, and evaluating methods of conducting and reporting communication research.

title: Instructional Communication
description: Seminar focusing on the effect of communication upon learning. For undergraduates interested in teaching or training. Emphasis on pedagogical principles and instructional materials. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Speaking Out for Community Change
description: Exploration of theory and practice in community advocacy. Focus on public deliberation, moral conflict, and community dialogue in value-laden topics and controversies. Prerequisites: CST 305 recommended for undergraduates.

title: Gender and Communication
description: Using a case study method, this course examines the relationship of gender to communication process. Identity, language, and relationships within cultural context are key concepts under study. Prerequisites: CST 207 or equivalent.

title: Health Communication
description: Focuses on current trends in the design of health messages. Explores health campaigns, interpersonal interventions, health care institutions, and consumer-based models and participatory approaches to health. Prerequisites: CST 105 . Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Communication Internship  3-6
description: Field learning experience using communication theory, research, and strategies in agencies and organizations within the larger community. Prerequisites: Will vary. junior or senior status, and permission of instructor; Notes: May be repeated for maximum of 6 s.h. credit. Open to majors only.

title: Family Communication
description: Exploration of family communication, including symbols, meanings, rules, traditions, stories, secrets, roles, artifacts, and theoretical frameworks. Practical application exercises and research related to family communication practices and patterns.

title: Communication and Ethnography: Writing Life
description: Intensive study and practice of methodologies of personal narrative, ethnography, autoethnography, and memoir. Examination and expression of self, identity, and communication in close personal relationships via writing life histories. CIC: CIC College Writing Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing.

title: Communication and Friendship
description: Course focuses on communication in friendships, via a close examination of friendships in everyday and popular culture contexts. Ethnographic methodology and dialectical, narrative, and identity theory frame our study. CIC: CIC College Writing

title: Environmental Communication
description: Explores environmental communication including current issues, theoretical frameworks, ethical frameworks, and the symbolic relationship of humans to nature. Emphasizes research, analysis, critical self-reflection, and practices.

title: African American Culture and Identity
description: Examines dynamic ways that African Americans construct, maintain, and negotiate their social identities in a variety of contexts. Explores the connections among race/ethnic identity, communication, and culture. Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing.

title: Internet and Society
description: Focusing on the intersections of communication and technology, this course introduces students to key debates and perspectives on how digital change influences everyday interactions and society.

title: Reclaiming Democracy: Dialogue, Decision-Making, Community Action
description: This course asks: How do we reclaim our democracy as a humane, inclusive process responsible to the needs of all community members and what does this require of us? Prerequisites: Junior or senior standing.

title: Relational Communication and the Hollywood Feature Film
description: Analysis and application of images, discourses, and practices concerning human communication and relationships as they are represented in the powerful cultural medium of film. Prerequisites: Undergraduates: CST 207 , CST 300 or CST 305 , and upper division standing.

title: Special Topics in Communication Research
description: Seminar in applying communication theory and research to current topics. Notes: May be repeated for credit an unlimited number of times when topics vary.

title: Senior Capstone
description: Graduating seniors synthesize their experience of the communication studies major by reflecting on course work and scholarly focus areas, assessing departmental learning outcomes, and constructing a career portfolio. Prerequisites: Senior status or permission of instructor.

title: Engaging Communication Scholarship I
description: Theories and research methodologies in communication studies. Emphasis on application of theory to social and cultural contexts and use of methods to solve research problems/answer research questions.

title: Engaging Communication Scholarship II
description: Study of theories and research methodologies in communication studies. Application of communication theory to social and cultural contexts and use of methods to solve research problems/answer research questions.

title: Instructional Communication
description: Seminar focusing on the effect of communication upon learning. For graduate teaching assistants in any discipline, graduates or undergraduates interested in teaching or training. Emphasis on pedagogical principles and instructional materials.

title: Communicating for Social Change
description: Theoretical and practical views on how to work inside and outside institutions of power to transform values, assert credibility, and mobilize others. Emphasis on how communication creates social change. Topics and emphasis vary by semester.

title: Speaking Out for Community Change
description: Exploration of theory and practice in community advocacy. Focus on public deliberation, moral conflict, and community dialogue in value-laden topics and controversies.

title: Communicating Innovation
description: Focuses on core communication theories, methods, and practices of advancing innovation, as well as public communication about health, science, and technology. Emphasizes multimedia storytelling and improving speaking and writing skills to communicate with public audiences about innovative ideas and navigating change.

title: Seminar in Communication Studies
description: Subject matter varies. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate degree program or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for credit.

title: Internet and Society
description: Examines how digital technologies reshape communication, combining historical perspectives with contemporary debates to help students analyze the evolving relationship between technology and society.

title: Communication, Organization, and Democracy
description: Draws upon critical organizational communication and critical/cultural studies to consider the role of communication in creating and sustaining democratic organizing in micro, meso, and macro contexts.

title: Communication Ethics and Social Justice
description: Theory and practice of communication ethics in a free society. Issues and cases ranging from intrapersonal to mass media communication situations. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Identity, Culture, and Communication
description: Takes critical/cultural studies approaches to examine how culturally significant identity sites such as race, ethnicity, gender, and diaspora are constructed, negotiated, and resisted through communication.

title: Independent Study   1-3
description: Topic to be determined by faculty advisor and student. Prerequisites: Permission of Instructor.

title: Research Practicum in Communication   1-3
description: Engagement in communication research with the direct supervision of a member of the Graduate Faculty. Prerequisites: Permission of Graduate Faculty member with whom student wishes to work. Notes: Grade: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory (S/U).

title: Seminar in Relational Communication
description: Major interpersonal communication theories and constructs, and methods for investigating them. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Capstone Experience in Communication (Final Research Project)   1-3
description: Synthesis of theories, research methods, and practices of communication that will shape the 21st Century, demonstrated through independent scholarly research conducted in consultation with faculty committee. Notes: Grade: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory (S/U).

title: Research Extension  1-3
description: Research Extension.

title: Leisure and American Lifestyles
description: Examination of personal, philosophical, socio-cultural, economic, behavioral, and historical dimensions of leisure; evolution of leisure lifestyles; exploration of the interrelationship between individuals, groups, and society in the context of leisure. MAC: MAC Health and Wellness Notes: Students may not receive credit for both CTR 101 and CTR 111.

title: Creating a Meaningful Life
description: Examine personal, social, and cultural bases for a healthy and well-balanced life. Recognize and foster creative potential for lifelong personal growth, satisfying quality of life through leisure, and meaningful rewards. MAC: MAC Health and Wellness

title: Sex, Drugs, & Rock n' Roll
description: An exploration of deviant leisure through the lens of recreational sex; substance use in recreational settings; and the use of music for forming relationships and establishing identity in leisure. MAC: MAC CritThink Soc and BehavSci

title: Fundamentals of Outdoor Leadership
description: Introduction to basic wilderness living skills, conservation of wild areas, sound safety practices, outdoor leadership theory, and practical application as related to a college outdoor recreation program. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Leadership in Recreation and Sport
description: Analysis of techniques, principles, and practices of leadership and group dynamics in recreation and sport.

title: Programming in Recreation and Sport
description: General principles of program planning in recreation and sport; analysis of methods and techniques of program design, organization, implementation, and evaluation.

title: Inclusive Recreation
description: Awareness of and sensitivity to the needs of people with disabilities and other marginalized groups with regard to planning, delivering, and evaluating recreation and sport services in the community. MAC: MAC CritThink Soc and BehavSci

title: Introduction to Recreational Therapy
description: Survey of key concepts, theoretical underpinnings, and procedures in clinical and community recreation settings. Focuses upon varied special needs populations, prescriptive activities, and documentations using medical charting.

title: Disabling Conditions and Recreational Therapy
description: Major primary and secondary disabling conditions typically encountered in RT practice will be covered. These include, but not limited to: physical disorders, behavioral health/psychiatric disorders, and cognitive/developmental disorders. Implications, considerations, and treatment techniques will be covered.

title: Introduction to Recreation and Sport
description: Operation of public, nonprofit, and commercial recreation and sport agencies, with emphasis on types of programs and services offered, current trends, and issues impacting delivery of services.

title: Fan Culture: Sport, Music, and Beyond
description: Exploration of fan culture, fandom, and fan communities as exemplified through sport, music, gaming, “influencers,” and beyond. This course focuses on developing a better understanding of subcultures and fan identity, as well as the relationships formed with fellow fans, the producer-consumer relationship, and divisions between rival fans. MAC: MAC CritThink Soc and BehavSci

title: Extreme and Lifestyle Sports
description: Examination of extreme and lifestyle sports, including the motivations for participation, the risks associated with participation, and the economy, careers, and cultures surrounding these activities. MAC: MAC CritThink Soc and BehavSci

title: Practicum Preparation in Recreation and Sport
description: This course prepares students for fieldwork in recreational therapy, recreation, and sport management by developing professional resumes, cover letters, job search strategies, and interview skills. Course must be passed with a C or higher. Prerequisites: RPMT majors or minors only.

title: Professional Internship Preparation in Recreation and Sport
description: This course prepares students for their CTR 417 / CTR 697 internship through interactive, hands-on activities that build confidence, professional skills, and understanding of internship expectations. Prerequisites: CTR 315 . RPMT majors.

title: Practicum in Recreation and Sport  3,6
description: A pre-internship course providing supervised, hands-on experience in recreation and sport settings, including opportunities for Recreational Therapy students to work with individuals with disabling conditions. Prerequisites: CTR 212 . CTR 213 ; CTR 214 or CTR 231 ; or permission of instructor; must have a cumulative 2.0 GPA to enroll; Notes: A three (3) semester hour experience may be repeated once for a maximum of 6 s.h.

title: Client Assessment in Recreational Therapy
description: Introduction to the theories, concepts, and techniques used in client assessment for recreational therapy programs and treatment. Prerequisites: CTR 231 or permission of instructor.

title: Contemporary Issues in Sport
description: This course examines the political, social, economic, and cultural dimensions of sport, exploring its role as a powerful medium for identity, expression, and societal issues through both historical and contemporary events.

title: Recreation and Sport Facility Development
description: Examination of the basic procedures involved in the planning process; basic considerations in park, recreation, and sport facility planning and maintenance management; analysis of the methods and techniques of site evaluation, design, and maintenance management for recreation and sport infrastructure.

title: Special Topics Seminar
description: Specific course title identified by subscript, e.g., Problems Seminar: Leisure and Aging. Nature of problems themselves and their impact on societies studied. Prerequisites: Course work in appropriate content area and/or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Financial Management in Recreation and Sport
description: Examination of the financial needs and techniques utilized in recreation and sport management. Emphasis on philosophy, terminology, and measurement of economies, revenue streams, and budgets.

title: Management in Recreation and Sport
description: Principles and practices of management in recreation, sport, and parks agencies with emphasis on motivation, leadership, finance and budgeting, personnel policies and practices, and marketing and public relations. Prerequisites: CTR 315 or permission of instructor.

title: Internship in Recreation and Sport
description: An advanced fieldwork course providing students with practical, hands-on experience in recreational therapy, recreation, and sport management settings. Prerequisites: CTR 315 and CTR 416 . must have a cumulative 2.0 GPA to enroll.

title: Evaluation Methods in Recreation and Sport
description: Analysis of evaluation methods in recreation and sport including the reasons for conducting evaluations, evaluation questions, data collection methods and analysis, interpretation of results for making recommendations, and report writing.

title: Directed Research in Recreation and Sport  1-3
description: Identification and investigation of research questions in recreation and sport. Opportunity for students to conduct research with direction from scholars in the field. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated twice for a maximum of 6 s.h.

title: Meeting and Event Planning and Management
description: Comprehensive understanding of the elements necessary to conduct a quality event or meeting; emphasis on skills needed to identify, create, organize, implement, and evaluate a special event, conference, or meeting.

title: Special Event Management
description: Study of elements necessary to manage and operate an event business; emphasis on development and presentation of event proposals, customer service, fee structures, event design, risk management, and legal issues.

title: Trends and Issues in Recreational Therapy
description: Study of trends and issues in recreational therapy; examination of current controversial issues, interdisciplinary team practice, supervisory functions, and professional advocacy. Prerequisites: CTR 315 , concurrent enrollment in CTR 439 or past enrollment in CTR 439 , or permission of instructor.

title: Animal Assisted Therapy
description: An introduction to the theories, concepts, and techniques used in animal assisted therapy in long-term care, rehabilitation, acute care hospitals, special schools, and other settings.

title: Interventions and Facilitation Techniques in Recreational Therapy
description: This course will prepare students to plan, implement, and evaluate a variety of recreational therapy interventions using various therapeutic facilitation techniques. Relevant efficacy research and theoretical foundations will be included. Prerequisites: Pr. or Coreq.: CTR 439 .

title: Clinical Procedures in Recreational Therapy
description: Students learn clinical skills related to the recreational therapy process with a focus on developing individualized treatment plans; developing goals, objectives and treatment interventions; documentation skills, discharge planning and evaluation. Prerequisites: Concurrent enrollment in CTR 231 or previous credit for CTR 231 . or permission of instructor.

title: Recreation, Parks, and Health
description: Examination of evidence regarding the impacts of recreation, parks, green space, and leisure on the health and well-being of individuals and communities.

title: Legal Aspects of Recreation and Sport
description: This course examines governmental structures and legal issues relevant to the delivery of recreation, park, and sport services. Political processes and court procedures that lead to legal precedents and determine public policy are covered. Selected topics include tort liability, intellectual property, contractual obligations, human rights, and risk management.

title: Honors Work  3-6
description: Honors Work. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. 3.30 GPA in the major; 12 s.h. in the major; Notes: May be repeated for credit if the topic of study changes.

title: Foundations of Leisure, Recreation, and Sport
description: Understand and apply various theories and concepts that influence the study of leisure behavior and the delivery of recreation and sport services.

title: Recreation and Sport Management
description: Theories and patterns of management appropriate for leisure service and sport delivery systems. Organizational planning, legal foundations, financial management, personnel management, and the politics of leisure service and sport delivery systems.

title: Financing and Budgeting in Recreation and Parks
description: Examination of the financial needs and techniques utilized in recreation and parks. Emphasis on philosophy, terminology, and measurement of economies, revenue streams, and budgets.

title: Professional Issues in Recreational Therapy
description: Study of professional issues in recreational therapy including professionalism, credentialing, research, professional preparation, continuing education, health care, ethics, advocacy, legislation, marketing, and role of recreational therapy managers in addressing these issues.

title: Advanced Procedures in Recreational Therapy
description: Health care delivery service in community and medical arenas. Role of recreational therapy within that system. Administrative/managerial procedures. Prerequisites: CTR 611 or permission of instructor.

title: Animal Assisted Therapy
description: An introduction to the theories, concepts, and techniques used in animal assisted therapy in long-term care, rehabilitation, acute care hospitals, special schools, and other settings.

title: Advanced Interventions and Facilitation Techniques in Recreational Therapy
description: Prepares students to select, plan, implement, and evaluate advanced recreational therapy interventions using various therapeutic facilitation techniques. Relevant efficacy research and theoretical foundations will be included. Prerequisites: CTR 611 or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Client Assessment in Recreational Therapy
description: Introduction to the theories, concepts and techniques used in client assessment for recreational therapy programs and treatment. Also addresses the administrative issues surrounding the development of an assessment plan and supervision issues surrounding the assessment process. Prerequisites: CTR 611 or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Clinical Procedures in Recreational Therapy
description: In this advanced clinical procedures course, students learn clinical skills related to the recreational therapy process with a focus on developing individualized treatment plans; developing clinical goals, objectives and treatment interventions; learning documentation, discharge planning, and evaluation skills. Prerequisites: CTR 611 or permission of instructor.

title: Recreation, Parks, and Health
description: Examination of evidence regarding the impacts of recreation, parks, green space, and leisure on the health and well-being of individuals and communities.

title: Community Development
description: Historical, sociological, and contemporary investigations of the concept of "community" and how it is used for bringing together disparate networks, negotiating resource allocation, group solidarity and collaboration, and civic engagement.

title: Independent Study
description: Independent study to support graduate-level research and demonstration projects under the close supervision of a graduate faculty member in CTR. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Directed Readings
description: Opportunity to conduct in-depth study and library work on a particular topic in recreation and parks under close supervision of a graduate faculty member in CTR. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Internship in Recreation and Sport  3-6
description: Advanced fieldwork course providing graduate students with practical, hands-on experience in recreational therapy, recreation, and sport management settings. Prerequisites: Written permission of instructor.

title: Field Project in Recreation and Sport  3-6
description: A supervised field project including a major literature-based paper. Prerequisites: Written permission of instructor.

title: Thesis  1-6
description: Individual guidance in the development and examination of a research problem. Prerequisites: Written permission of instructor.

title: Thesis Extension  1-3
description: Thesis Extension.

title: Research Extension  1-3
description: Research Extension.

title: Principles of Autonomy and Agency
description: Introduction to principles of autonomy and agency with emphasis on personal responsibility in relationship to self, peers, and community. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Introduction to Personal Finance
description: Introduction to basic financial literacy skills including assessment of personal resources, budgeting and goal setting, and prioritization and/or tracking of personal spending habits. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Academic Success Planning
description: Introduction to student-centered academic planning and goal setting with an emphasis on strategies for success in a college environment. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Financial Services, Providers, and Products
description: Designed to increase knowledge of the different characteristics and functions of financial services and providers in the U.S., along with exploration of personal relationship to banking. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Introduction to Dimensions of Campus and Community
description: Introduction to exploring a new campus community and making the most out of what it has to offer socially and recreationally, given individual interest and desires. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student. Notes: May be repeated for a total of 6 s.h.

title: First Year Practicum
description: Course designed to enhance student's understanding of personal management and success through experiential learning activities. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Introductory Independent Study
description: Course is designed to provide the student with an introductory opportunity to participate in the creation of academic learning experiences geared to individual needs, interests, aptitudes, and desired outcomes through and independent academic project.

title: Community Engagement
description: Through this course, students will explore and apply knowledge of the relationship between personal autonomy and agency in the context of community engagement. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Financial Security on a Budget
description: Expanding knowledge of personal finance methods and strategies for maximizing financial resources as a student in college. Prerequisites: CTP 106 . Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Navigating College Life
description: Interactive seminar designed to build on student's knowledge of personal responsibility skills required for successful navigation of life on campus and in community. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Holistic Success Planning
description: Provides opportunities for deeper engagement with person-centered planning process through class-led meetings and activities. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Career Exploration
description: Exploration of personal interests and strengths related to career goals: resume building, informational interviewing, and other discovery-related activities. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Comprehensive Transition and Postsecondary Education
description: Follow-up to CTP 158 , with emphasis on personal reflection and development of ability to adapt to a variety of social and recreational activities and/or settings in the greater community. Prerequisites: CTP 158 . Integrative Community Studies student; Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Internship Preparation
description: Professional preparation and planning for 156 hour internship experience. Prerequisites: CTP 257 . Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Sophomore Practicum
description: Course designed to give students an opportunity to further develop personal management and success through experiential learning activities. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Independent Study
description: Course is designed to provide the student with an opportunity to participate in the creation of academic learning experiences that are foundational to the ICS program of study through an independent academic project.

title: Essential Elements of Credit Management
description: Expanding knowledge of credit building and management skills including ability to protect oneself against identity theft, impact of bankruptcy, and other concepts related to long-term financial stability. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Directing Your Resources
description: Interactive seminar designed to broaden knowledge of philosophy of self-direction and practical strategies for building self-directed supports in the community. Prerequisites: CTP 224 . CTP 314; or permission of instructor; Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Personal Financial Planning Seminar
description: Interactive seminar providing overview of taxpayer rights and responsibilities, tax regulations, and expansion of asset building strategies. Culmination of CTP finance curriculum offering basis for development of post-college financial plan. Prerequisites: CTP 106 . CTP 206 ; CTP 306 ; Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Junior Practicum
description: Course designed to enhance student's understanding of personal management and success through experiential learning activities in their junior year at UNCG.

title: Advanced Independent Study
description: Course is designed to provide the student with an opportunity to participate in the creation of academic learning experiences through the development of an advanced independent academic project that furthers learning in a desired area of interest.

title: Internship
description: Practical experience in the desired career field. Prerequisites: CTP 257 . CTP 267 ; Integrative Community Studies student; Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Senior Seminar
description: Interactive seminar that focuses on the development of presentation skills and strategies for the effective use of the senior capstone portfolio as a tool for sustained self-direction. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student. Notes: May be repeated for a total of 6 s.h.

title: Senior Practicum
description: Guided practice of activities designed to increase self-direction skills. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student.

title: Senior Capstone Portfolio
description: Development of senior portfolio that outlines student progress and overall command of the learning outcomes within the program of study. Prerequisites: Integrative Community Studies student. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Internship and Job Development
description: Practical experience in desired career field with emphasis on development of skills necessary to identify job-related needs and negotiate with potential employers. Prerequisites: CTP 257 . CTP 267 ; CTP 393 ; Integrative Community Studies student; Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Algorithm Analysis and Design
description: An examination of topics in algorithm design and analysis including sequential algorithm design and complexity analysis, dynamic programming, greedy algorithms, and graph algorithms. Also covers selected advanced topics from NP-completeness; approximation, randomized, parallel, number-theoretic algorithms; Fast Fourier Transform; computational geometry; and string matching.

title: Data Science
description: Problem-based learning introduction to data science, including  programming with data; data mining, munging, and wrangling; statistics, analytics, and visualization towards scientific, social, and environmental challenges.

title: Big Data and Machine Learning
description: Big data definitions and characteristics, computing environment for big data management and processing, machine learning models and algorithms, and scaling up machine learning (high dimensionality reduction).

title: Capstone Project in Computational Analytics
description: Capstone course. Students work with local industries and nonprofit organizations to solve important data science problems under the supervision of a mentor.

title: The Beauty and Joy of Computing
description: A broad-based introduction to key concepts and principles of computer science. Exploration of seven big ideas of computing: creativity, abstraction, data, algorithms, programming, the Internet, and impact of computing.

title: Experimental Course  1-6
description: This number reserved for experimental courses. Refer to the Course Schedule for current offerings.

title: Introduction to Computer Concepts
description: Introduction to computers and computing. Topics cover impact of computers on society, ethical issues, hardware, and software applications.

title: Data, Computing, and Quantitative Reasoning
description: Problem-based introduction to quantitative reasoning, including computational methods; formulation of quantitative arguments; algorithmic understanding, selection, and utilization; data modeling, interpretation, and summarization of results, on real world datasets. MAC: MAC Quantitative Reasoning

title: Computational Problem Solving
description: Using computing to apply mathematical concepts in developing algorithmic solutions to real-world problems, stressing analysis and logical reasoning. A modern programming language will be introduced for examples and assignments. Prerequisites: Non-Computer Science majors only or permission of instructor.

title: Introduction to Computer Programming for Non-Majors
description: Introduction to computer programming for non-computer science majors, including programming concepts of variables, expressions, decision statements, iteration, functions, and modular design. Language and applications chosen to be relevant to non-majors.

title: Exploring Artificial Intelligence: Concepts and Practice
description: This course is designed for non-CS major students and covers concepts and applications of AI and societal impacts and ethical considerations of AI. Selected advanced topics such as generative AI and large language models will also be delivered with hands-on experience. No programming knowledge is required. MAC: MAC Nat Sci Data Analysis

title: Introduction to Computer Science
description: Programming in a high-level language. Emphasis on problem analysis, problem-solving techniques, and software design principles and techniques. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in MAT 110 , MAT 112 , MAT 115 , MAT 118 , MAT 120 , MAT 150 , MAT 151 , MAT 181 , MAT 183 , MAT 184 , MAT 190 , MAT 191 , or MAT 196 .

title: Elementary Data Structures-A Transition
description: Comparison between Java and other high level languages. Advanced syntax of Java. Emphasis on modularization and abstraction. Big-O analysis of algorithms. Design and use of abstract data types with various implementations. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 120 or permission of the instructor.

title: Elementary Data Structures and Algorithms
description: Advanced syntax of high level language taught in CSC 130 . Emphasis on modularization and abstraction. Big-O analysis of algorithms. Design and use of abstract data types with various implementations. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 130 .

title: Foundations of Computer Science I
description: An introduction to the fundamental ideas underlying contemporary computer science with a focus on the computation and construction of objects. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 130 or permission of instructor.

title: Computer Organization and Assembly Language
description: Introduction to the organization of the computer through the use of Assembly Language programming. Data representation, parts of the computer system, Assembly Language fundamentals, instruction sets, memory, and floating-point operations. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 130 or permission of instructor.

title: Ethics in Computer Science
description: Historical and social context of computing, ethical responsibilities of the computing professional, intellectual property rights, and risks and liabilities. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 230 and in CSC 250 , or permission of instructor. Notes: Computer Science majors only.

title: Advanced Data Structures
description: Static and dynamic data structures emphasizing binary trees and graphs. Advanced programming techniques. Advanced sorting and searching algorithms. Hashing techniques. Performance analysis. Methods of developing large applications programs. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 220 or CSC 230 and in CSC 250 or MAT 253 .

title: Concepts of Programming Languages
description: Concepts of block-structured, object-oriented, functional, logic, and concurrent programming languages. Comparative study of syntactic and semantic features of these languages and writing programs using them. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 330 .

title: Software Engineering
description: Practical and theoretical concepts of software engineering. CIC: CIC College Writing Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 330 .

title: Foundations of Computer Science II
description: High level concepts in the theoretical foundations of computer science. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 250 , or permission of instructor.

title: System Programming
description: System programming with emphasis on processes, memory management, multithreaded programming, synchronization and deadlocks, interprocess communication, parallel and distributed computing, networking, files systems, security, signals, and virtualization containers. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in CSC 230 and CSC 261 , or permission of instructor.

title: Web Development
description: Introduction to concepts of web application development, including web page structure and appearance, client-side interactivity and behavior, web services and APIs, server-side scripting, persistent data storage, and software version control. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in CSC 230 and CSC 261 , or permission of instructor.

title: Data Science
description: Problem-based learning introduction to Data Science, including  programming with data; data mining, munging, and wrangling; statistics, analytics, and visualization, towards scientific, social, and environmental challenges. Prerequisites: A grade of B+ or better in CSC 330 and ( STA 271 or STA 290 ), or permission of instructor (prior programming and statistics experience is required).

title: Network Analysis
description: Concepts and methods of network analysis, including network data extraction, management, model, visualization, and analysis of network structure and dynamics. Prerequisites: Grade C or better in CSC 330 , or permission of instructor.

title: Big Data and Machine Learning
description: Big data definitions and characteristics, computing environment for big data management and processing, machine learning models and algorithms, and scaling up machine learning (high dimensionality reduction). Prerequisites: A grade of C or better in CSC 330 and ( STA 271 or STA 290 ), or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Data Science
description: Experiential learning towards advanced concepts of Data Science, including efficient and parallel programming with large scale datasets, advanced data organization and storage, applied machine learning and inferencing, towards real-world challenges. Prerequisites: A grade of B+ or better in CSC 405 and CSC 410 .

title: Computer Graphics
description: Survey of graphics algorithms, data structures, and techniques. Prerequisites: Grades of at least C (2.0) in CSC 340 , CSC 350 , and either MAT 292 or MAT 296 , or permission of instructor.

title: Digital Image Processing
description: Image representation, enhancement, compression, coding, restoration, and wavelet transforms. Prerequisites: Grades of at least C (2.0) in CSC 330 , CSC 350 , and either MAT 292 or MAT 296 , or permission of instructor. Successful completion of STA 271 or STA 290 recommended.

title: Deep Learning in Computer Vision
description: Concepts of state-of-the-art deep learning architectures, algorithms for various computer vision tasks (e.g., such as image classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, image generation, and high-dimensional image analysis), and hands-on exercises and project. Prerequisites: Grade C or better in CSC 330 , or permission of instructor.

title: Bioinformatics
description: Introduction to the problems and methods in Bioinformatics. Problem areas include restriction mapping, map assembly, sequencing, DNA arrays, and sequence comparison. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor.

title: Numerical Analysis and Computing
description: Number systems and errors, solutions of non-linear and linear systems, interpolation, numerical differentiation and integration, solution of differential equations. Implementation of numerical methods using a high-level programming language. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in MAT 293 or MAT 296 , or permission of instructor.

title: Artificial Intelligence
description: Logical foundations, knowledge representation and reasoning, search, and selected topics such as natural language processing and reasoning under uncertainty. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 330 and CSC 350 or permission of instructor.

title: Introduction to Compiler Design
description: Basic techniques of compiler design and implementation: lexical analysis, parsing, code generation. Sizable programming project implementing a compiler for a block-structured language with strong typing. Prerequisites: Grades of at least C (2.0) in CSC 261 and CSC 330 or permission of instructor. Notes: Successful completion of CSC 432 helpful.

title: Human-Computer Interface Development
description: Survey of concepts and techniques for human-computer interface development. Topics include user-centered design, user interface programming, and usability evaluation. Prerequisites: Grades of at least C (2.0) in CSC 340 or permission of instructor.

title: Natural Language Processing
description: Word representation, text preprocessing techniques, concepts of state-of-the-art deep learning architectures, algorithms for various NLP tasks (named entity recognition, question answering, information extraction, sentiment analysis etc.), and hands-on exercises and project. Prerequisites: Grade C or better in CSC 330 or CSC 350 , or permission of instructor.

title: Theory of Computation
description: Finite state automata and regular expressions, context-free grammars, push-down automata and their use in parsing, overview of language translation systems, models for programming language semantics, computability and undecidability. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 350 . or permission of instructor.

title: Algorithm Analysis and Design
description: Sequential algorithm design and complexity analysis. Dynamic programming. Greedy algorithms. Graph algorithms. Selected advanced topics from NP-completeness; approximation, randomized, parallel, number-theoretic algorithms; Fast Fourier Transform; computational geometry; string matching. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 330 .

title: Principles of Computer Architecture
description: Hardware and software components of computer systems, their organization and operations. Topics: comparative instruction set architectures, microprogramming, memory management, processor management, I/O, interrupts, and emulation of processors. Prerequisites: Grades of at least C (2.0) in CSC 261 , CSC 330 , and CSC 350 , or permission of instructor.

title: Principles of Operating Systems
description: Techniques and strategies used in operating system design and implementation: managing processes, input/output, memory, scheduling, file systems, and protection. Prerequisites: Grades of at least C (2.0) in CSC 340 and CSC 362 or permission of instructor. Notes: Successful completion of CSC 461 helpful.

title: Principles of Database Systems
description: Contemporary database systems. Emphasis on query processing, design, and implementation of applications in relational (SQL) databases. Introduction to other database models such as XML, object-oriented, and deductive. Prerequisites: Grade of at least C (2.0) in CSC 330 , or permission of instructor.

title: Principles of Data Mining
description: Foundation and principles of data mining-Architecture, languages, data preparation and core algorithms including association mining, classification and clustering will be discussed. Topics such as graph mining may also be covered. Prerequisites: CSC 471 or permission of instructor.

title: Principles of Computer Networks
description: Hardware and software components of computer networks, their organization and operations. Topics: open system interconnection; local area networks; TCP/IP internetworking, routing, and packet switching; network programming. Prerequisites: Grades of at least C (2.0) in CSC 261 and CSC 330 , or permission of instructor.

title: Principles of Wireless Networks
description: Digital communications, communication networks, wireless communication technology, wireless networking, wireless LANs, and wireless network programming. Prerequisites: Grades of at least C (2.0) in CSC 330 and CSC 477 , or permission of instructor.

title: Principles of Computer Security
description: Core concepts in computer security, including the security goals of confidentiality, integrity, and availability; authentication; access control; security software development; use of cryptography; and basic network security. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in CSC 330 and CSC 362 , or permission of instructor.

title: Modern Cryptography
description: Theory and practice of cryptography, emphasizing formal models and security reasoning. Primitives covered include private and public-key encryption, message authentication codes, hash functions, digital signatures, secret sharing, and zero-knowledge proofs. Prerequisites: A grade of C or better in CSC 481 , or permission of instructor.

title: Network Security
description: The course explores the network security concepts of communication protocols; security in routing; remote authentication; access policies; web security; network vulnerabilities; intrusion detection and prevention; and network traffic analysis. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in CSC 481 or permission of instructor.

title: Senior Capstone
description: Application of classroom knowledge and skills in computer science to solve real-world problems and to develop research and development skills. CIC: CIC College Writing Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. student must be in the final semester of major coursework.

title: Directed Study in Computer Science  1-3
description: Directed Study in Computer Science.

title: Honors Work in Computer Science
description: Research in a topic of special interest at the Honors level. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for credit when topic changes.

title: Directed Study in Computer Science  1-3
description: Notes: Grade: Pass/Not Pass (P/NP).

title: Selected Topics in Computer Science
description: A topic of special interest is studied in depth. Prerequisites: Junior standing and permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for credit for a total of 6 s.h. when topic of study changes.

title: Data Science
description: Problem-based learning introduction to Data Science, including  programming with data; data mining, munging, and wrangling; statistics, analytics, and visualization, towards scientific, social, and environmental challenges. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor (prior programming and statistics experience is required).
