description: An examination of the differences between acting on film and acting on stage. Students will act in a variety of different types of camera work including feature films, dramas, sitcoms, and commercials. Prerequisites: THR 231 . Theatre Majors only.

title: Performance Styles
description: Introduction to acting styles such as Greek, Commedia dell'Arte, Shakespeare, and Comedy of Manners, with pedagogical opportunity to lesson-plan for additional genres and styles. Prerequisites: THR 232 . Drama major.

title: Advanced Acting for the Camera Studio
description: An advanced study of performance for the camera. Camera scene study will continue with added emphasis on clarity of intention and accuracy of repetition. Prerequisites: THR 332 . B.F.A. in Acting and B.F.A. in Musical Theatre students only.

title: Professional Theatre Audition Techniques
description: Audition theory, techniques, and practice for theatre, television, and films. Prerequisites: THR 332 . B.F.A. and M.F.A. Actors only; or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Stagecrafts
description: Study of advanced scenic construction, property construction, and rigging techniques for the stage. Supervised laboratory work in wood, plastics, metal, and other materials. Prerequisites: THR 242 . or permission of instructor.

title: Technical Direction
description: Theatrical technical direction with emphasis on organizational, managerial, and problem-solving duties and responsibilities. Lecture combined with practical projects. Prerequisites: THR 140 and THR 140L , and THR 344 . or permission of instructor.

title: Scene Painting I
description: Tools, materials, and techniques of scene painting. Prerequisites: THR 242 . Design and Technical Production concentration; or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Scene Painting
description: Advanced practice of scenic art techniques and their incorporation into design as integral components of theatrical expression. Emphasis will be given to the creation of pictorial representations, variations in layout practices, and incorporation of stencils. Prerequisites: THR 442 or permission of instructor.

title: Professional Practices in Design and Technical Production
description: In this course, students evaluate their portfolio materials and explore topics related to standard theatre practices. Students leave this course with a Launch Plan, laying out their post-graduation goals and plans to achieve them. Discussion topics may include freelancing, graduate school, design and tech unions, hiring practices, and adjacent industries. Prerequisites: THR 248 . Open only to those in the BFA Drama with Design and Technical Production concentration; Senior status; or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced CAD for the Theatre
description: Continuation of Computer Aided Drafting for the Theatre concentrating on 3-D virtual modeling and visualization and its use in the theatre. Prerequisites: THR 344 or permission of instructor.

title: Stage Millinery and Accessories
description: Historical millinery and accessories in relation to stage adaptation; design and construction techniques. Prerequisites: THR 145 / THR 145L . or permission of instructor.

title: Costume Pattern Drafting and Draping
description: Advanced garment production processes and practices for stage and film with an emphasis on pattern creation, manipulation, fitting, alterations. Introduction of specific construction techniques for creating historical garments for performance. Preparation for careers in costume related fields. Prerequisites: THR 244 . or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Scene Design
description: Advanced problems in set design for performance. Topics may vary with each offering. Prerequisites: THR 351 , THR 444. BA or BFA in DRAM; or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Costume Design
description: Advanced problems in costume design for performance. Topics may vary with each offering. Prerequisites: THR 361 , THR 449 . BA or BFA in DRAM; or permission of instructor.

title: Makeup Special Effects
description: Students will explore the principles, materials and techniques of making and applying 3 dimensional special makeup effects through hands-on projects that focus on physiognomy and principles of design. Develop skills in sculpting, special effects prosthetic application, mold-making, lifecasting and makeup painting techniques. Prerequisites: THR 262 or permission of instructor.

title: BFA Performance Senior Seminar: Preparing to Showcase Your Talents Post Graduation
description: Preparing the graduating theatre artist for a New York showcase and assisting them in the transition from college to their first year as a professional actor. Prerequisites: THR 332 .

title: Advanced Lighting Design
description: Advanced problems in lighting design for performance. Topics may vary with each offering. Prerequisites: THR 370 and THR 344 . BA or BFA in DRAM; or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Stage Management
description: A seminar-style course exploring the artistic and interpersonal dimensions of stage management. Students build on foundational skills to examine leadership, collaboration, and conflict resolution. Through case studies, discussion, and project-based learning, students prepare for professional roles by analyzing and applying advanced practices in production environments. Prerequisites: THR 386 . Restricted to School of Theatre majors and minors.

title: Playwriting II
description: Focus on advanced play construction and dramatic writing. Prerequisites: THR 380 .

title: Teaching Methods in Theatre Arts
description: Philosophy, means, and methods for conducting classes and structuring curricular and cocurricular theatre activities. Includes internship in public schools as lab. Required for Theatre Education teacher licensure.

title: Honors Work  3-6
description: Honors Work. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. 3.30 GPA in the major, 12 s.h. in the major; Notes: May be repeated for credit if the topic of study changes.

title: Theatre Management
description: Theatre organization and operation. Practical problems of financing, promoting, and staffing various theatre programs. Prerequisites: B.A. and B.F.A. Theatre Majors, or permission of the instructor.

title: Directing Practicum
description: Independent practice in directing the play; preparations of a 25-minute play or excerpt, and presentation in the Workshop Theatre. Benchmarks include project proposal prior to registration, extended five-page directorial statement with dramaturgy, auditions, rehearsal scheduling, single faculty-observed off-book rehearsal, dress/tech rehearsals, performance, and post-mortem formal paper. Prerequisites: THR 410 . B.F.A. Drama, Theatre Education K-12 Teaching Licensure concentration, or permission of the instructor; Notes: May be repeated for a total of two semester hours.

title: Theatre Management and Entrepreneurship
description: Learn how to develop and manage a theatre in the twenty-first century. This class provides fundamental knowledge and critical thinking skills to prepare students for a career in Theatre Management. Students will actively engage with the most relevant challenges faced by leaders in contemporary Theatre/Entrepreneurship. Prerequisites: None. Notes: Students who have prior credit for ENT 486 may not take THR 486 for credit.

title: Theatre Field Studies  1-3
description: Guided off-campus study in the Theatre disciplines. Travel required. Site and topic will vary from semester to semester. Notes: May be repeated for up to four (4) semester hours.

title: Experiential Theatre Project II  1-4
description: Intensive experience in one or more areas of theatre production. Prerequisites: B.A. and B.F.A. Drama Majors. THR 291; or permission of the instructor.

title: Developing and Managing Educational Programs for Arts Organizations
description: Using a case study approach, students will learn about how education departments at arts organizations operate in the 21st century from both theoretical and practical perspectives. Prerequisites: Must be majoring in a CVPA degree track or a School of Education degree track. Junior standing or above; Notes: Same as AAD 489 .

title: Advanced Theatre Practice-Performance
description: Departmental auditions are open to all students, and credit is given to those who are cast. Prerequisites: Written permission required. Notes: May be repeated up to six (6) times for credit.

title: Advanced Theatre Practice-Scenery
description: Open to non-Design and Technical Production students interested in participating in the scenery phase of production. Prerequisites: THR 140 / THR 140L . or permission of the instructor; Notes: May be repeated for credit.

title: Advanced Theatre Practice-Costumes
description: Open to non-Design and Technical Production students interested in participating in the costuming phases of production. Prerequisites: THR 145 / THR 145L OR THR 147/ THR 147L . or permission of the instructor; Notes: May be repeated for credit.

title: Advanced Theatre Practice - Theatre Management
description: A study of theatre management through hands-on work for the School of Theatre. Students will work in the areas of box office, marketing, ushering, house management, community engagement, education, and development based on their preferences and the needs of the School of Theatre. Prerequisites: Junior standing. or permission of the instructor; Notes: May be repeated for credit.

title: Advanced Theatre Practice-Production
description: Open to non-Design and Technical Production students interested in participating in the technical phases of production. Prerequisites: THR 140 / THR 140L OR THR 145/ THR 145L OR THR 146/ THR 146L OR THR 147/ THR 147L . Permission of Instructor; Notes: May be repeated for credit.

title: Advanced Theatre Practice-Lighting
description: Open to non-Design and Technical Production students interested in participating in the lighting phase of production. Prerequisites: One (1) s.h. of THR 191 and junior standing. or permission of instructor; Notes: May be repeated for credit.

title: Advanced Theatre Practice - Stage Crew
description: Open to any student interested in participating in the stage crew operations of production including run crew, wardrobe crew, lighting crew or other positions backstage. Prerequisites: THR 140 / THR 140L , THR 145 / THR 145L , THR 146 / THR 146L or THR 147 / THR 147L . or permission of instructor; Notes: May be repeated three times for credit.

title: Student Teaching in Theatre Arts
description: Capstone experience for theatre education majors: student teaching at elementary, middle, or high school levels; classroom management; discipline; lesson design and implementation; and assessment. Includes biweekly seminars. Prerequisites: Grade of B- or better in THR 482 . completion of all major and teacher licensure course work; and completion of 100 hours of pre-student teaching field experience in K-12 schools.

title: Capstone Production Practicum
description: Capstone experience for a BFA DRAM major: Design and Technical Production concentration student. Prerequisites: THR 140 or THR 145 or THR 146 or THR 147 . junior standing and BFA DRAM major: Design and Technical Production concentration; or permission of instructor.

title: Theatre History I
description: Specific conditions under which the great plays of the world have been produced. Origins through the late French Renaissance up to the English Restoration.

title: Theatre History II
description: Specific conditions under which the great plays of the world have been produced. The English Restoration and German Romanticism through Modern and Postmodern movements.

title: Theatre History III
description: Specific conditions under which the great plays of the world have been produced. I: Origins to Renaissance; II: Early Modern to Realism; III: Modernism to Postmodernism.

title: Styles in Performance Design I
description: Survey of historical architecture and decor styles from antiquity to the present and how they are used in design for performance. Prerequisites: Admission to M.F.A. in Drama or permission of instructor.

title: Styles in Performance Design II
description: Survey of historical clothing and accessories from antiquity to the present and how they are used in design for performance. Prerequisites: Admission to M.F.A. in Drama or permission of instructor.

title: American Theatre History
description: Development of Theatre in America from its beginnings to the present. Includes consideration of theatre artists and companies, audiences, performance spaces, production and business practices, plays, and playwrights.

title: Global Performance and Theatre
description: Cultural performance and theatre around the world engage elements of music, dance, storytelling, ritual, drama, and so much more. Investigate dramatic texts and performance from different global contexts to expand comprehension of theatre as a global phenomenon. Subject varies with instructor.

title: Feminist Theatre
description: Survey of twentieth and twenty-first century British and American feminist theatre, focusing on performance texts that address salient concerns of first, second, and third wave feminists.

title: Directing II
description: An intermediate directing course that involves the student in the theory and practice of contemporary plays. Prerequisites: Two theatre courses and THR 201 and THR 410 . or permission of instructor.

title: Drama in Education
description: Research and literature of drama strategies and techniques for children, ages 5 through 14. Practice in leading groups of children in drama in educational settings. Prerequisites: M.F.A. Theatre majors or permission of the instructor.

title: Puppetry
description: Scope and development of puppetry throughout the world. Practical experience in the design, making, and performing of puppets. Prerequisites: M.F.A. Theatre Majors or permission of the instructor.

title: Devised Theatre
description: Research and literature of devised theatre pedagogy and methodology. Practice in facilitating devised theatre in education and in the community. Prerequisites: M.F.A. Theatre majors or permission of the instructor.

title: Advanced Shakespeare Studio
description: Study and practice of Shakespearean acting styles. Performance emphasizing manners, movement, and vocal delivery of the Elizabethan period. Prerequisites: M.F.A. Acting concentration only.

title: Professional Theatre Audition Techniques
description: Audition theory, techniques, and practice for theatre, television, and films. Prerequisites: M.F.A. Actors concentration only or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Stagecrafts
description: Study of advanced scenic construction, property construction, and rigging techniques for the stage. Supervised laboratory work in wood, plastics, metal, and other materials.

title: Technical Direction
description: Theatrical technical direction with emphasis on organizational, managerial, and problem-solving duties and responsibilities. Lecture combined with practical projects.

title: Scene Painting I
description: Tools, materials, and techniques of scene painting.

title: Advanced CAD for the Theatre
description: Continuation of Computer Aided Drafting for the Theatre concentrating on 3-D virtual modeling and visualization and its use in the theatre. Prerequisites: THR 444 or permission of instructor.

title: Stage Millinery and Accessories
description: Historical millinery and accessories in relation to stage adaptation; design and construction techniques.

title: Costume Pattern Drafting and Draping
description: Targets students preparing for careers in costume related fields. Emphasis will be placed on pattern creation, manipulation, fitting, alterations, and construction techniques for creating historical garments for performance.

title: Theatre Management
description: Theatre organization and operation. Practical problems of financing, promoting, and staffing various theatre programs. Prerequisites: M.F.A. Theatre Major, or permission of the instructor.

title: Directing Practicum
description: Practice in directing the play; preparations of a 25-minute play or excerpt, and presentation in the Workshop Theatre. Prerequisites: THR 410 and M.F.A. Theatre Majors, or permission of the instructor. Notes: May be repeated for a total of two semester hours.

title: Theatre Field Studies  1-3
description: Guided off-campus study in the Theatre disciplines. Travel required. Site and topic will vary from semester to semester. Notes: May be repeated for up to four (4) semester hours.

title: Experiential Theatre Project II  1-4
description: Intensive experience in one or more areas of theatre production.

title: Variable Topics in Theatrical Design
description: An intensive study of practices in design for the performing arts. Topics vary each semester. Notes: May be repeated once for credit when topic varies.

title: Variable Topics in Technical Production
description: An intensive study of topics in technical production for the performing arts using interdisciplinary skills. Topics vary by semester. Notes: May be repeated once for credit when topic varies.

title: Applied Theatre II  1-4
description: Intensive experience in one or more areas of theatre production. Prerequisites: THR 291. B.A., B.F.A., and M.F.A. Theatre Majors, or permission of the instructor.

title: Drama Theory and Criticism
description: Major concepts representative of Aristotelian drama and the chief artistic and philosophical trends underlying much of contemporary or non-Aristotelian drama. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for a maximum of 3 hours credit.

title: Seminar in Drama and Theatre History
description: Investigation of special topics in drama and theatre history. Topics vary each semester. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated twice for credit when topic varies.

title: Acting Seminar
description: Historical and theoretical perspectives related to acting. Topics vary each semester. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for a maximum of 9 hours credit.

title: Broadway: Development of the American Musical
description: Exploring Musical Theatre's development from European tradition to a uniquely American art form. Places operetta, minstrelsy, vaudeville, burlesque, revue, and musical comedy into historical context. Illustrated lectures and demonstrations.

title: Musical Theatre Literature and Repertoire
description: Study of musical theatre literature and repertoire. Students apply their understanding of the genre by making song assignments for undergraduate musical theatre students and rehearsing/staging the selected material.

title: Theatre for the Very Young
description: Theatre for the Very Young (TVY) explores the global history of TVY and the evolution of the field in the United States focusing on youth ages 0-5.

title: Applied Play Directing
description: Guided study and practice in developing directorial approaches and techniques through scene work and the production of a complete play. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Play Directing
description: Problems of play directing emphasizing the use of modified proscenium, arena, thrust, and experimental forms of stages. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Directing Period Plays
description: Theories of directing and their application in the direction of period plays. Prerequisites: THR 610 or THR 611 and admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Directing Seminar
description: Investigation in the art of directing. Topics vary each semester. Prerequisites: THR 610 or THR 611 and admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated for credit.

title: Collaboration in Theatrical Production
description: An examination of the creative working relationship between theatre artists onstage and off. Students develop their abilities to articulate, experiment with, synthesize, and integrate best practices in theatrical collaboration. Rotating topics may include introduction to pedagogy, reflexive practices, creative problem solving, and ethical research practices. Prerequisites: M.F.A. in Drama major or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Drama in Education
description: Current trends in advanced philosophies in drama in education and aesthetic education. Practical experience arranged with classes in local schools. Prerequisites: THR 515 and admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Trends in Theatre for Young Audiences
description: Focus on analysis of nationally successful Theatre for Youth companies. Exploration of seasons, budgets, and outreach components of various companies. Prerequisites: THR 517 , THR 584 , THR 615 .

title: Graduate Seminar in Music Direction
description: Synthesis of a well-rounded music director for theatrical production. Lecture-based course that speaks to differing topics, challenges, and methods of Music Direction for the stage. First part of two-semester trajectory.

title: Advanced Graduate Seminar in Music Direction
description: Synthesis of a well-rounded music director for theatrical production. Lab-based course that relies on practical application of topics from THR 618 with faculty oversight/guidance. Second part of two-semester trajectory. Prerequisites: THR 618 .

title: Stage Dialects
description: Stage dialects most frequently used by the actor: Standard British, Cockney, Irish, Scots, French, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Brooklynese, American Southern. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Advanced Dynamics: Movement
description: Movement laboratory for the advanced actor. Prerequisites: Admission to the appropriate degree program (MFA in Drama, Acting Concentration). Notes: May be repeated for a total of six credits.

title: Advanced Dynamics: Voice
description: Voice and speech training to develop skills necessary for performance. Prerequisites: Admission to MFA in Drama, Acting Concentration. Notes: Three consecutive semesters to be repeated for a total of six credits.

title: Advanced Physical Violence for the Stage
description: Advanced study and practice in the mechanics of stage violence. Designed for graduate actors, this course will focus on safe performance techniques for violence in period and contemporary texts. Prerequisites: Admission to M.F.A. in Drama, Acting Concentration.

title: Black and Indigenous People of Color Plays in Theatre for Youth
description: Black and Indigenous People of Color (BIPOC) Plays in Theatre for Youth explores literature in Theatre for Youth written by people of color. Includes scripts, playwrights, and productions. Prerequisites: MFA Drama.

title: Studies in Acting
description: Special studies and practices in acting techniques. Emphasis on contemporary drama. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Contemporary Acting II
description: Contemporary ensemble acting techniques and their application to ensemble style and playing. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Period Acting II
description: Study and practice of acting styles from the periods of Classical Greek, Roman, Medieval, Restoration/Georgian, Victorian/Edwardian drama. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Acting for the Camera
description: An introductory examination and exploration of the technique of acting for the camera. Students will refine and adjust the way they work, but the core technique remains the same. Prerequisites: M.F.A. Actors or permission of the instructor.

title: Advanced Acting for the Camera Studio
description: An advanced study of performance for the camera. Camera scene study will continue with added emphasis on clarity of intention and accuracy of repetition. Prerequisites: THR 332 or THR 534, M.F.A. Actors only.

title: Graduate Design Foundations
description: An introduction to scholarship and research methodologies in the School of Theatre as well as resources available through the Graduate School and the University. Students will explore topics related to theatrical design, creative problem solving, academic writing, narrative based art, scholarly research, and health and well-being in the entertainment industry. Prerequisites: Admission to M.F.A. in Drama, Design Concentration or permission of instructor.

title: Scenic Automation and Technology
description: Introduction to mechanical devices used for automation of stage scenery. Topics covered will include stage winches, fluid power (hydraulics and pneumatics), control systems, and system design. Prerequisites: Admission to M.F.A. in Drama, Design concentration or permission of instructor.

title: Theatre Properties and Crafts
description: Explores advanced research, communication, and construction of theatrical properties with an emphasis on historical accuracy. Students complete hands-on projects tied to assigned productions and develop prop shop skills while practicing mentorship and leadership within the shop environment. Prerequisites: Admission to M.F.A. in Drama, Design concentration or permission of instructor.

title: Developing and Managing Educational Programs for Arts Organizations
description: Using a case study approach, students will learn about how education departments at arts organizations operate in the 21st century from both theoretical and practical perspectives.

title: Advanced Theatre Graphics
description: Theatre graphics for performance design, drafting, perspective, figure drawing, and rendering.

title: Costume Crafts
description: Advanced exploration of design elements and fabric properties in relation to costuming, with a focus on critical analysis and innovative application. Laboratory projects emphasize advanced use of materials and technologies for costume craft projects that focus on creative application, surface design, and 3D construction.

title: Portfolio Preparation for the Theatre and Academia
description: This course guides students in developing the materials and skills necessary to present their education and experience to employers in the most effective manner, including resumes, cover letters and portfolios.

title: Scene Design Seminar
description: Topics in scene design. Topics may include conceptual development, drawing and rendering techniques, model building, and generation of computer graphics. Prerequisites: Admission to MFA in Drama, Design Concentration. Notes: This course may be repeated once for 3 credits when topic varies.

title: Scene Design Styles
description: Topics in Scene Design Applications. Topics may include study of theatrical genres and variations in physical space and relationship to audiences. Prerequisites: Admission to MFA in Drama, Design Concentration.

title: Scene Design Application
description: Topics in Scene Design Applications. Topics may include design conceptualization and development, professional standards for drafting and elevations, rendering and models. Prerequisites: THR 655 and admission to MFA in Drama, Design Concentration.

title: Costume Design Seminar
description: Topics in costume design. Topics may include conceptual development, drawing and rendering techniques, and generation of computer graphics. Prerequisites: Admission to MFA in Drama, Design Concentration. Notes: This course may be repeated once for 3 credits when topic varies.

title: Introduction to Wig Making
description: Introduction to common wig making techniques such as ventilation and wefting, lace front construction, simple facial hair sets, and how to take a head tracing for more accurate wig and facial hair construction.

title: Costume Design Styles
description: Topics in Costume Design Styles. Topics may include varying dramatic genres, studies of costume style presentation appropriate for performance trends. Prerequisites: Admission to MFA in Drama, Design Concentration.

title: Costume Design Application
description: Topics in Costume Design Applications. Topics may include design conceptualization and development, as well as professional standards for illustrating action figures, specific fabrics, and developing costume production charts. Prerequisites: THR 665 and admission to the MFA in Drama, Design Concentration.

title: Lighting Design Seminar
description: Topics in lighting design. Topics may include technology of moving fixtures, techniques of image and effect projection, and techniques of lighting for film. Prerequisites: Admission to MFA in Drama, Design Concentration or permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated once when topic varies for a total of 6 credit hours.

title: Lighting Design Styles
description: Advanced problems of lighting design for theatrical productions in performance. Studies can include studio design exploration in the different genres of plays, musicals, and opera. Prerequisites: Admission to MFA in Drama, Design Concentration or permission of instructor.

title: Lighting Design Application
description: Creative topics in lighting design emphasizing collaborative approaches to produced work for public performance. Prerequisites: THR 675 and admission to MFA in Drama, Design Concentration or permission of instructor.

title: The Art and Craft of Playwriting
description: Theory and practice of writing plays for stage production. Prerequisites: Permission of instructor. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Seminar in Scenography
description: Scenographic theory and practice for performance design.

title: Graduate Design for Theatre
description: Topics in theatrical design through the lens of Access, Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (ADEI). Students will critically evaluate how their design choices impact both artists and audiences, and will create inclusive, equitable environments for theatrical storytelling. Topics may include conceptual development, drawing and rendering techniques, and ethical research practices.

title: Graduate Design for Theatre
description: Deepens students' understanding of theatrical design across cultural and historical contexts. Emphasizing leadership and collaboration, students translate diverse perspectives into visual storytelling while refining their artistic voice through self-reflection, critique, and adaptability to evolving production demands. Topics may include design conceptualization and development, professional standards, and professional reflexivity.

title: Graduate Design for Theatre
description: Students will conduct advanced design research, explore new techniques, and execute a major project that demonstrates technical mastery and artistic excellence. Emphasizing leadership, communication, and flexibility, the course prepares students to navigate real-world production challenges while upholding ADEI principles and fostering a healthy, collaborative community.

title: Graduate Practicum in Theatre
description: Guided practice under rigorous artistic standards in carrying out major responsibilities in UNCG Theatre or Theatre for Youth major productions. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program and permission of instructor.

title: Graduate Practicum in Costume Design
description: Guided practice under rigorous artistic standards in carrying out major costume design responsibilities in the School of Theatre's production season or other faculty approved venues. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program.

title: Graduate Practicum in Lighting Design
description: Guided practice under rigorous artistic standards in carrying out major lighting design responsibilities in the School of Theatre's production season or other faculty approved venues. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program.

title: Graduate Practicum in Scene Design
description: Guided practice under rigorous artistic standards in carrying out major scene design responsibilities in the School of Theatre's production season or other faculty approved venues. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program.

title: Independent Study   1-3
description: Prerequisite: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor, major advisor and School Director.

title: Advanced Experimentation
description: Preparation for the evaluation of experiences in the creative processes of theatre, broadcasting, or film. Analysis, documentation, and critical evaluation of the specific experience. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Design Colloquium
description: Explore the theatrical design process through realized designs, culminating in fully produced work in the School of Theatre or in collaboration with university and/or community partners. Features group discussions, lectures, and mentoring related to practical applications of theatrical design, reasonable industry practices, and physical and mental well being.

title: Graduate Internship
description: Field experience in local production companies, post-production houses, television stations or professional theatre companies. Academic supervision provided by faculty and directed activity in the field provided by site supervisor. Prerequisites: Second or third year standing in the graduate program.

title: Master Production in Theatre
description: Research, development, and execution in acting, directing, or design of a major theatrical production. Prerequisites: Admission to appropriate School of Theatre degree program or permission of instructor.

title: Information Literacy for Health and Wellness
description: An introduction to information literacy skills and how to apply them to health information. This course will serve as a bridge course for students who transfer certain two credit health and wellness courses into UNCG. MAC: MAC Health and Wellness Prerequisites: Successful completion of a two credit MAC health and wellness course at another institution and Instructor permission.

title: Online Students Succeed at the G
description: This course is designed to help online campus students transition successfully to academic and student life. Students will learn and connect with the UNCG community, campus resources and opportunities, while developing skills essential for academic success, and beginning their journey to discovering their own purpose and potential. MAC: MAC Foundations Prerequisites: None. Notes: UNS 100 is for online degree seeking students only. Must have fewer than 60 credits or can enroll by written permission.

title: Experimental Course  1-6
description: This number reserved for experimental courses. Refer to the Course Schedule for current offerings.

title: Succeed at the G
description: This course helps new students transition successfully to academic and student life. Students connect with the UNCG community, campus resources and opportunities, while developing skills essential for academic success, and beginning their journey to discovering their own purpose and potential. Topics addressed supplement discipline content, major, and professional development. MAC: MAC Foundations Prerequisites: None. Notes: UNS 101 is open to all majors. Must have fewer than 60 credits or can enroll by written permission.

title: Exploratory Students Succeed at the G
description: This course is designed to help students who are undecided on their major transition successfully to academic and student life. Students will connect with the UNCG community, campus resources and opportunities, and develop skills essential for success in any field, while beginning their journey to discovering their purpose and potential. MAC: MAC Foundations Prerequisites: None. Notes: UNS 105 is only for students who are undecided in their major. Must have fewer than 60 credits to enroll.

title: Nursing Students Succeed at the G
description: This course helps direct admission Nursing students transition successfully to academic and student life. Students connect with the UNCG community, campus resources and opportunities, while developing skills essential for academic success, and beginning their journey to discovering their own purpose and potential. Topics addressed supplement discipline content and professional progression. MAC: MAC Foundations Prerequisites: None. Notes: UNS 110 is for direct admission Nursing students only. Must have fewer than 60 credits to enroll.

title: Business Students Succeed at the G
description: This course helps new Business students transition successfully to academic and student life. Students connect with the UNCG community, campus resources and opportunities, while developing skills essential for academic success, and beginning their journey to discovering their own purpose and potential. Topics addressed supplement discipline content, major, and professional progression. MAC: MAC Foundations Prerequisites: None. Notes: UNS 115 is for new Business Major students only. Must have fewer than 60 credits to enroll.

title: Experimental Course  1-6
description: This number reserved for experimental courses. Refer to the Course Schedule for current offerings.

title: Lead at the G
description: This course is designed to help student leaders develop the leadership skills needed to foster academic success and serve as effective peer mentors for new students in transition. Student leaders will learn about key resources and explore topics related to student development theory, leadership theory, pedagogy, and personal development. Prerequisites: Written Permission. Notes: All students will be pre-enrolled in the course. Grade: Pass/Not Pass (P/NP).

title: Introduction to Videogame and Esports Studies
description: Introduces students to videogame and esports studies. Students examine the three components of the field (videogame studies, videogame production/design, and esports) and learn how to examine each critically. No background in gaming is required and access to special technology is not necessary. MAC: MAC CritThink Hum and Fine Art

title: Critical Approaches to Videogaming and Esports Studies
description: This course analyzes video gaming and esports cultures and values, as well as how they interact with society in general. This course teaches students the history, methodology, and theoretical approaches to videogaming and esports from various fields and disciplines.

title: Writing for Games
description: Offers students the opportunity to hone their professional writing skills so they can be prepared to enter the videogaming and esports industry. Students examine academic writing, games journalism, videogame narratives, curricula, and other documents that prepare them to compose various texts for differing audiences. CIC: CIC College Writing

title: Research Methodologies for Videogaming and Esports Studies
description: Developing a capstone project takes support and an understanding of the available resources. This 7-week course prepares students for their capstone. Students will engage with videogame and esports studies, as well as honing research, writing, and speaking skills. Students will meet virtually with the professor and conduct asynchronous peer review.

title: Videogaming and Esports Studies Capstone
description: This class supports students completing a capstone project. The class has three virtual meetings; primarily, students work independently and meet with the professor as needed. The final product will be a creative project that showcases the student’s skills and academic abilities; students will deliver two formal presentations showcasing their work. Prerequisites: VES 401 .

title: Advanced Game Studies: History and Theory
description: This course explores the evolution of games as expressive media, tracing their development from early analog and digital forms to contemporary interactive experiences. Students will engage with games as texts, systems, and experiences, developing a nuanced understanding of their place in media history and theory. CIC: CIC College Writing Prerequisites: VES 230 .

title: Body Mapping: What Every Performer Needs to Know About the Body
description: Introduces the concept of body mapping (our mental representations of structure, function, and size) and provides a weekly laboratory for "updating" these maps during practical activity and experimental movement. Prerequisites: Freshman, sophomore, or junior standing.

title: The Arts as Human Experience
description: An examination of the meaning of the arts experience, including its historical and personal significance. Includes reading and related work in art, dance, drama, and music. MAC: MAC CritThink Hum and Fine Art

title: The Alexander Technique for Performers
description: An active and scholarly exploration of the Alexander Technique to unlock creativity, discover freedom and ease in performance, reduce stress and tension throughout the body, and prevent related injuries. MAC: MAC Health and Wellness

title: The Arts as Social Change: Theory and Practice
description: Students will examine the ways in which the arts create social change in our institutions and communities through an exploration of theory and case studies, and an engagement in practice.

title: Dance for Musical Theatre
description: For students interested in developing dance skills to be used specifically for the musical theatre genre, including tap, ballet, jazz and contemporary. Various pieces of choreography will be set in class.

title: Exploring Visual and Performing Arts Cultures  1-3
description: The course is used for Art, Music, Theatre, and Dance study trips. Offered only when a course accompanies a specific trip. Prerequisites: Determined by needs of specific trip. Notes: Grade: Pass/Not Pass (P/NP).

title: Musical Theatre Audition Technique II
description: For undergraduate students interested in the continued and advanced development of their musical-theatre audition skills. Prerequisites: THR 357 or permission of instructor.

title: Community Arts Methods
description: This course explores a range of community arts methods including community research and relationship building, project development and logistical planning, social practice methods, developmentally appropriate teaching strategies, and curriculum development. Prerequisites: VPA 360.

title: Community Arts Intensive
description: In 10 full days of collaborative workshops with UNCG faculty and guest artists, immersive creative experiences, and site visits with community partners, students will develop a tool-kit of specific strategies and evaluation methods as they workshop, troubleshoot, and refine their ideas for community arts projects and programs.

title: Community Arts Internship
description: During this course, students will implement a previously planned community arts project with an established community partner, under supervision of UNCG faculty. Prerequisites: VPA 361 .

title: Research Methods in Social Practice
description: This interdisciplinary course explores research methods in Social Practice, an art form that employs diverse strategies for participatory and experiential art within a range of contexts, spaces and media. Notes: May be repeated twice for credit.

title: Independent Study / Directed Research  1-3
description: Independent directed study or research with faculty supervision in an area of special interest.

title: Research Methods in Social Practice
description: This interdisciplinary course explores research methods in Social Practice, an art form that employs diverse strategies for participatory and experiential art within a range of contexts, spaces and media. Notes: May be repeated twice for credit.

title: The Alexander Technique for Performers
description: An active and scholarly exploration of the Alexander Technique to unlock creativity, discover freedom and ease in performance, reduce stress and tension throughout the body, and prevent performance-related injuries.

title: Exploring Visual and Performing Arts Cultures  1-3
description: Course used for Art, Music, Theatre, and Dance study trips. Offered only in conjunction with a specific trip. Prerequisites: To be determined by needs of specific trip.

title: Western Civilization
description: Interdisciplinary study of Western Civilization emphasizing critical developments from ancient to modern times.

title: WGSS Foundations: Claiming Your Education
description: This course invites students to explore how gender is shaped within institutions and how these structures impact individuals, providing critical tools to examine, and claim, their education. MAC: MAC Foundations

title: Gender, Race, and Sexuality
description: Students will address the complex historical and contemporary relationships among gender, race, and sexuality as important and interdependent aspects of social life.

title: Philosophy of Race and Gender
description: This course provides historical background for understanding racial inequalities, focuses on the concepts of race and gender and how they are connected to racism and sexism, and explores the concept of intersectionality. MAC: MAC CritThink Hum and Fine Art Notes: Same as PHI 224 .

title: An Introduction to Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies
description: An interdisciplinary introduction to frameworks and approaches in the field of women's, gender and sexuality studies. MAC: MAC CritThink Hum and Fine Art

title: Writing in Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies
description: Focuses on the development of writing skills for the discipline of Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies. Fosters information literacy, proficiency in engaging with and responding to scholarship, and the cultivation of strong research practices. CIC: CIC College Writing Prerequisites: WGS 250 or WGS 270 .

title: Introduction to LGBTQI Studies
description: An introduction to the academic study of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, and intersex histories, experiences, and cultures. MAC: MAC CritThink Hum and Fine Art

title: Women's Health and Bodies
description: How politics and culture shape ideas about diverse women's health and bodies. Topics include gender, race, sexuality, disability, reproduction, disease, violence, and the health care system. MAC: MAC Health and Wellness

title: Women, Gender, and Freedom in U.S. History
description: An exploration on how gender has shaped understandings of democracy in the United States. The course uses gender, cultural, and historical analysis to interpret and analyze some of the nation’s foundational texts and to explore their continued legacies. MAC: MAC Civics and Community

title: Christianity and the Construction of Gender
description: Study of the role of categories of male and female in the creation and maintenance of Christian notions of holiness, authority, and hierarchy in historical perspective. CIC: CIC College Writing Notes: Same as REL 310 .

title: Gender and Violence
description: An exploration of gender-based violence in contemporary culture and social life. Topics discussed may include assault, stalking, intimate partner violence, and media portraits of gender violence. Notes: Students cannot receive credit for both WGS 311 and SOC 419 or WGS 419 .

title: Sociological Perspectives on Gender
description: Inquiry into status of genders in society with emphasis on structural and institutional relationships as well as socialization and the continuities and discontinuities in gendered displays across the life cycle. Notes: Same as SOC 329 .

title: Postcolonial and Transnational Feminisms
description: Examines histories of, and critical feminist responses to, western colonialism and imperialism. Explores the construction of race, gender, class, and sexuality within imperial projects and anti-imperial movements. CIC: CIC College Writing Prerequisites: WGS 250 or WGS 270 .

title: Latin American and Latinx Feminist and Queer Thought
description: This course invites students to learn about Latin American Feminist and Queer thought, its social impact, and related transnational networks from the 17th century until today. It will also include connections to the ideas and activism of Latinx feminists of the last century in the United States.

title: Introduction to Feminist Theories
description: Explores and evaluates feminist theories in a socio-historical context. Raises questions about their implications for different methods of inquiry and about the nature of knowledge and rational thought. CIC: CIC College Writing Prerequisites: WGS 250 or WGS 270 .

title: Ecofeminism
description: Study of Ecofeminism as a philosophy and social movement that focuses on interconnections between feminism and environmentalism. Analysis of paradigms and inequalities that devalue and oppress both 'women' and 'nature'.

title: Topics in Native American and Indigenous Studies
description: Exploration of the literatures, cultural productions, histories, and politics of indigenous peoples. Topic will vary depending on section. Notes: May be repeated for credit when topic varies. Same as ENG 375 .

title: Independent Study  1-3
description: Intensive independent study of specialized topics. Prerequisites: Permission of sponsoring instructor. Notes: May be repeated once for credit.

title: Gender, Crime, and Deviance
description: Examines key questions in scholarly literature on gender, crime, and deviance: how gender socialization, gender roles, and institutions affect males' and females' offending, deviant behavior, and victimization. Prerequisites: None. Notes: Same as SOC 419 . Students cannot receive credit for both SOC 419 and WGS 311 .

title: Critical Sexuality Studies and Queer Theory
description: Examines sexuality as an identity and an object of knowledge in connection to race, class, and gender. Includes exploration of the history of, and new developments in, queer theory. Prerequisites: WGS 350 or permission of instructor.

title: Topics Seminar in Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies
description: An in-depth study of a selected topic or topics in Women's, Gender, and Sexuality Studies involving directed reading and research. Notes: May be repeated up to three (3) times for credit as topics vary.

title: Internship in Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies
description: Practical experience in a variety of professional settings. Includes biweekly seminar with internship coordinator. Students complete at least 7-10 hours a week at internship sites. Prerequisites: WGS 250 , plus 6 additional hours in WGS. 6 hours in core courses in major, if other than WGS; Notes: For Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies majors and minors only.

title: Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies Senior Capstone Course
description: Senior-level work on a major theme in the field of Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies. Each student will design and complete a culminating research project. CIC: CIC College Writing Prerequisites: Women's, Gender and Sexuality Studies Major.

title: Honors Work  3-6
description: Prerequisite: Permission of instructor; 3.30 GPA in the major, 12 s.h. in the major; Notes: May be repeated for credit if the topic of study changes.

title: Independent Study   3-6
description: A research or creative project related to women's and gender studies supervised by a WGS faculty member. Prerequisites: At least 9 hours with a B (3.0) average or better.

title: Women's, Gender, and Sexuality Studies Internship
description: Supervised experience related to women's, gender, and sexuality studies. May include teaching internship, service learning opportunity, applied research experience, or internship in social justice organizations and agencies. Prerequisites: For master's students, at least 15 credit hours of graduate-level course work including WGS 650 and WGS 651 .

title: Internship Seminar
description: Prerequisite: At least 15 credit hours of graduate level course work including WGS 650 and WGS 651 ; Corequisites: WGS 601 .

title: Feminist Theory: Intersections of Gender, Race and Class
description: Introduces feminist social movements across historical and global contexts. Relies on interdisciplinary lenses and epistemologies, particularly as contested identity politics intersect with other systems of power and relationships.

title: Feminist Research Analysis
description: Feminist approaches to philosophical and practical inquiries: data collection, analysis, and presentation. Prepares students to critically read research to understand the place of gender in lived experiences and institutional spaces. Prerequisites: WGS 650 .

title: Race, Gender, and U.S. Empire
description: Interdisciplinary investigation of the social construction of race and gender in the context of U.S. warfare and imperial statecraft. Examines critiques of US empire using critical race and feminist frameworks.

title: Culminating Project
description: A capstone experience for the master's degree in women's and gender studies. Students design and present culminating project in their area of specialization and professional portfolio developed from WGS courses.

title: Thesis   1-6
description: Individual guidance in the development of a specific research problem. Notes: May be repeated for a maximum of 6 hours credit.

title: Critical Sexuality Studies and Queer Theory
description: Examines sexuality as an identity and an object of knowledge in connection to race, class, and gender. Includes exploration of the history of, and new developments in, queer theory.

title: Topics in Gender and Sexuality Studies
description: Intensive work at an advanced level on a selected topic in Gender and Sexuality Studies. Notes: Same as ENG 706 . May be repeated once for credit when topic varies.

title: Thesis Extension  1-3
description: Thesis Extension.

title: Research Extension   1-3
description: Research Extension.
